topic: First matching minimax LOWER bound for Neyman regret in adaptive two-arm experiments, resolving the open problem of Dai et al. (arXiv:2411.14341 Logarithmic Neyman Regret): they prove the upper bound R_T <= O~(f(pi*) log T) for the Clipped-Second-Moment-Tracking design but prove no matching lower bound. Establish the minimax lower bound over bounded-outcome adaptive two-arm instances via a change-of-measure / epoched two-point (Le Cam) construction tying detection-time of the unknown second moments to accumulated quadratic Neyman loss V(pi_t)-V(pi*), pinning the SHARP minimax Neyman-regret rate (accumulated variance-inefficiency of the adaptive Horvitz-Thompson ATE estimator vs the oracle Neyman allocation pi*=S1/(S0+S1)); exact overlap dependence determined by the lower-bound proof, not fixed in advance. Distinct from simple-regret/best-arm minimax (arXiv:2309.08808, 2512.08513) which concern chosen-arm welfare, not estimator variance-suboptimality.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove the first matching minimax lower bound for cumulative Neyman regret in adaptive bounded-outcome two-arm ATE experiments, matching logarithmic upper bounds for tracking-style adaptive Neyman allocation.

source_refs.claim: The paper frames the gap as nonasymptotic adaptive ATE estimation: prior asymptotic work overlooks the difficulty of learning the optimal allocation, and ClipSMT achieves O(log T) Neyman regret but gives no matching minimax lower bound.
source_refs.author: Ojash Neopane, Aaditya Ramdas, Aarti Singh

source_refs.claim: The paper introduces Neyman regret because adaptive-design precision gains are not sufficiently understood even in two-arm settings, and proves an O~(sqrt(T)) upper bound for Clip-OGD.
source_refs.author: Jessica Dai, Paula Gradu, Christopher Harshaw

source_refs.claim: The paper studies adaptive sequential designs whose efficiency approaches the hindsight-optimal nonadaptive design and proves anytime O~(log T) Neyman regret.
source_refs.author: Georgy Noarov, Riccardo Fogliato, Martin Bertran, Aaron Roth

open_problems.why_unsolved: The literature now has logarithmic upper bounds for adaptive Neyman regret and asymptotic efficient-inference results for adaptive ATE estimation, but no theorem of the form inf_algorithm sup_instance E R_T >= c * complexity(instance) * log T for the same accumulated variance-inefficiency objective. Nearby minimax lower bounds for adaptive experiments target treatment choice or best-arm welfare, so their loss is the final chosen-arm regret rather than the cumulative excess variance V(pi_t)-V(pi*) of an adaptive Horvitz-Thompson ATE estimator.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp minimax lower-bound theorem over bounded two-arm potential-outcome distributions proving Omega(log T) cumulative V(pi_t)-V(pi*) against every adaptive allocation rule, with the instance-complexity factor determined by the lower-bound construction.

writing_exemplar.why: It gives the cleanest motivation arc for the Neyman-regret objective, bounded outcomes, Horvitz-Thompson variance, and the logarithmic upper-bound claim.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: ATE estimation in adaptive experiments needs precision, not welfare maximization -> oracle Neyman allocation minimizes HT variance but depends on unknown second moments -> ClipSMT gives logarithmic Neyman-regret upper bounds -> the matching minimax lower bound for this variance-regret objective is missing.

method_exemplars.technique: Change-of-measure minimax lower bounds for adaptive two-arm experiments with Neyman-allocation variance learning, followed by CLT and large-deviation upper-bound comparisons.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It supplies the nearest adaptive-experiment lower-bound machinery using standard-deviation learning, but its regret is treatment-choice welfare rather than cumulative estimator-variance loss.

method_exemplars.technique: Information-theoretic minimax lower bounds for bounded-reward off-policy evaluation under importance-weighted estimation risk.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is an estimation-risk lower-bound analogue for inverse-propensity estimators; the new proof must add adaptive logging chosen to learn pi*.

method_exemplars.technique: Online convex optimization analysis of cumulative Neyman regret for adaptive Horvitz-Thompson ATE estimation.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It defines the oracle comparison and regret functional that the lower bound must match.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine the exact overlap and second-moment dependence in the logarithmic minimax Neyman-regret lower bound, instead of inheriting possibly loose polynomial factors from upper-bound analyses.

source_refs.claim: The paper emphasizes improving T-dependence from sqrt(T) to log(T) and reducing exponential dependence on problem parameters to polynomial dependence.
source_refs.author: Ojash Neopane, Aaditya Ramdas, Aarti Singh

source_refs.claim: The paper notes that Neyman allocation cannot be implemented without known standard deviations and studies multistage estimation of those standard deviations.
source_refs.author: Jinglong Zhao

source_refs.claim: The classical allocation rule minimizes stratified-estimator variance by allocating in proportion to arm or stratum standard deviations.
source_refs.author: Jerzy Neyman
source_refs.venue: Journal of the Royal Statistical Society

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing upper bounds carry clipping, overlap, second-moment, and lower-moment conditions as algorithmic regularity parameters. They do not say which of these factors are unavoidable when pi* is close to 0 or 1, when one arm has small second moment, or when the curvature of V(pi) around pi* makes small allocation errors expensive. A lower-bound proof has to optimize the indistinguishability of second-moment perturbations against the quadratic Neyman loss they induce.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A parameterized minimax lower-bound theorem giving the sharp complexity factor multiplying log T as a function of pi*, S0, S1, overlap, and the curvature/information tradeoff for estimating arm-specific second moments.

writing_exemplar.why: It is the closest paper on problem-parameter dependence in logarithmic Neyman regret.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Neyman allocation depends on unknown second moments -> clipping and tracking make logarithmic regret possible -> upper bounds expose overlap and moment factors -> the minimax proof must decide which factors are information-theoretic.

method_exemplars.technique: Clipped second-moment tracking with finite-sample bounded-outcome concentration and parameter-dependent regret accounting.
method_exemplars.why_closest: Its constants and clipping thresholds identify the candidate dependence that a sharp lower bound must either match or refute.

method_exemplars.technique: Multistage variance-estimation analysis for adaptive Neyman allocation under an information-limit comparison.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It isolates standard-deviation learning as the bottleneck, although its competitive-analysis target is not cumulative HT variance regret.

method_exemplars.technique: Distribution-local lower-bound comparisons that track standard-deviation ratios in two-arm Neyman allocation.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It shows how Neyman-allocation parameter dependence enters a minimax proof, but for simple regret rather than Neyman-regret accumulation.

open_problems.open_problem: Separate the fixed-design, design-based minimax Neyman-regret rate from the superpopulation rate, and prove the matching lower bound in the fixed finite-population potential-outcomes model.

source_refs.claim: The paper works in a design-based potential-outcomes framework and seeks an adaptive design nearly as efficient as the infeasible nonadaptive Neyman design with access to all potential outcomes.
source_refs.author: Jessica Dai, Paula Gradu, Christopher Harshaw

source_refs.claim: The paper's logarithmic analysis is superpopulation, while it contrasts Dai et al. as the more challenging fixed-design setting.
source_refs.author: Ojash Neopane, Aaditya Ramdas, Aarti Singh

source_refs.claim: The paper introduces unequal-probability inverse-weighted estimation and the variance formulas underlying design-based HT-style ATE estimation.
source_refs.author: Daniel G. Horvitz, Donovan J. Thompson
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: The strongest logarithmic regret guarantees are stated in a superpopulation setting, while the original Clip-OGD problem is design-based with fixed potential outcomes revealed only through adaptive treatment assignment. Fixed-design lower bounds cannot rely on the same independent resampling structure, and the current literature does not settle whether the minimax rate is logarithmic, square-root, or governed by another finite-population detection barrier.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-population lower-bound theorem constructing hidden bounded potential-outcome tables such that every adaptive design has a specified cumulative excess HT variance relative to the hindsight Neyman allocation.

writing_exemplar.why: It is the clearest design-based formulation of adaptive Neyman allocation and the hindsight finite-population oracle.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: Adaptive sequential designs can improve ATE precision -> design-based Neyman regret compares to a hindsight finite-population oracle -> known algorithms give upper bounds but not a sharp converse -> fixed-design minimax lower bounds must reveal the rate.

method_exemplars.technique: Design-based adaptive Horvitz-Thompson variance decomposition and Neyman-regret accounting.
method_exemplars.why_closest: The lower bound would target exactly the fixed-design oracle comparison and variance functional introduced there.

method_exemplars.technique: Adaptive reweighting arguments showing how data-dependent propensities create skewness, heavy tails, and coverage problems for IPW/AIPW estimators.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It identifies finite-sample inference pathologies caused by adaptive propensities, which are the same mechanism a fixed-design lower bound can exploit.

method_exemplars.technique: Design-based inverse-probability weighting and finite-population variance formulas under unequal assignment probabilities.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It provides the variance object whose adaptive excess is accumulated in Neyman regret.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend sharp minimax lower bounds from Horvitz-Thompson Neyman regret to estimator-aware adaptive designs such as AIPW, contextual, and multigroup Neyman regret while preserving valid inference.

source_refs.claim: The paper frames AIPW adaptive Neyman allocation as minimizing variance against an oracle over assignment probabilities and linear predictors, identifies nonconvexity as central, and proves a T^{-1/2}R minimax rate under its conditions.
source_refs.author: Fangyi Chen, Shu Ge, Jian Qian, Christopher Harshaw

source_refs.claim: The paper studies efficient ATE inference in sequential experiments with changing treatment policies, giving CLTs and anytime confidence sequences for adaptive AIPW.
source_refs.author: Thomas Cook, Alan Mishler, Aaditya Ramdas

source_refs.claim: The paper introduces contextual multigroup Neyman regret, requiring an adaptive design to outperform each group's best nonadaptive design.
source_refs.author: Georgy Noarov, Riccardo Fogliato, Martin Bertran, Aaron Roth

open_problems.why_unsolved: Recent papers extend adaptive Neyman ideas in different directions: AIPW and covariates, anytime-valid inference, and contextual multigroup guarantees. The lower-bound landscape is therefore fragmented: Chen et al. prove a minimax rate for their design-based AIPW setting, while contextual or multigroup HT-style regret and inference-compatible estimator-aware designs do not yet have a unified sharp minimax characterization comparable to the desired two-arm logarithmic lower bound.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A hierarchy theorem of minimax lower bounds stating when covariate adjustment, outcome-model learning, or multigroup constraints reduce or increase the information needed to learn the oracle allocation, together with a compatible CLT or confidence-sequence condition for the resulting estimator.

writing_exemplar.why: It cleanly states estimator-aware Neyman regret for AIPW, includes an oracle variance formulation, and already contains a minimax lower-bound result to contrast with HT and multigroup variants.
writing_exemplar.motivation_arc: HT Neyman regret treats assignment probabilities as the design object -> AIPW adds outcome-model choices and nonconvex oracle variance -> contextual and multigroup constraints add competing oracle comparisons -> sharp minimax lower bounds must identify which extra structure changes the rate while keeping inference valid.

method_exemplars.technique: Martingale CLTs, adaptive AIPW estimating equations, and anytime-valid confidence sequences under changing policies.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It shows how adaptive efficiency guarantees must coexist with valid inference for the same causal estimand.

method_exemplars.technique: Reduction of nonconvex AIPW Neyman regret to simultaneous convex regrets plus a matching lower-rate argument.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It is the nearest estimator-aware minimax Neyman-regret proof template.

method_exemplars.technique: Anytime multigroup Neyman-regret analysis over overlapping covariate groups.
method_exemplars.why_closest: It defines the contextual regret target whose minimax lower bounds remain open.

literature_map:
1. Neyman 1934: classical optimum allocation minimizes estimator variance by allocating proportional to standard deviations.
2. Horvitz and Thompson 1952: unequal-probability inverse-weighted estimation and variance formulas underpin HT ATE variance objectives.
3. Response-adaptive randomization and clinical RAR reviews frame data-dependent allocation as an efficiency/ethics tradeoff, but not as sharp minimax Neyman regret.
4. Hahn-Hirano-Karlan-style adaptive propensity-score design motivates learning variance-minimizing treatment probabilities.
5. Kato, Ishihara, Honda, and Narita 2020 studies asymptotically efficient adaptive ATE design, but not sharp nonasymptotic regret.
6. Hadad, Hirshberg, Zhan, Wager, and Athey 2019/2021 and Waudby-Smith et al. 2022 address inference/OPE under adaptivity, not allocation-regret lower bounds.
7. Dai, Gradu, and Harshaw 2023 define Neyman regret in a design-based adaptive Neyman-allocation problem and prove O~(sqrt(T)) expected regret.
8. Zhao 2023 studies multistage adaptive Neyman allocation and the difficulty of unknown arm standard deviations under competitive analysis.
9. Neopane, Ramdas, and Singh 2024 arXiv:2411.14341 is Logarithmic Neyman Regret; the arXiv authors are Neopane/Ramdas/Singh, not Dai et al., and it gives O(log T) ClipSMT upper bounds without a matching minimax lower bound.
10. Noarov, Fogliato, Bertran, and Roth 2025 strengthen design-based Neyman-regret guarantees and introduce contextual multigroup Neyman regret.
11. Kato 2024 arXiv:2412.17753 and Kato 2025 arXiv:2512.08513 prove minimax lower bounds for best-arm or treatment-choice simple regret, useful as change-of-measure templates but aimed at welfare rather than estimator variance inefficiency.
12. Cook, Mishler, and Ramdas 2023/2024 develop semiparametric efficient inference and confidence sequences for adaptive experiments.
13. Chen, Ge, Qian, and Harshaw 2025/2026 extend adaptive Neyman allocation to AIPW estimators and prove a design-based minimax rate in that estimator-aware setting.
prior_proposal_map: No direct prior proposal for Neyman-regret minimax lower bounds or Neyman allocation was found in local formalization artifacts. The closest reusable map is by proof component: stat_ate_overlap_decay supplies unresolved weak-overlap ATE lower-bound/frontier channels; stat_policy_regret_margin_overlap supplies a regret lower-bound versus feasible-tightness residual; exp_rollout_chebyshev_minimax and exp_design_pm1_exactness_boundary supply design-variance minimax and exact-covariance/frontier residuals; DML/Riesz proposals supply ATE variance/report frontier gaps; dynamic IV compliance supplies adaptive-experiment assignment-memory evidence. These were not cross-linked because they do not name the same Neyman-regret lower-bound problem as the web scan.
